subject: Physics
q: The heat radiated per unit area in 1 hour by a furnace whose temperature is $3000 \mathrm{~K}$ is $(\sigma=5.7 \times$ $10^{-8} \mathrm{~W} \mathrm{~m}^{-2} \mathrm{~K}^{-4}$ )
question: The heat radiated per unit area in 1 hour by a furnace whose temperature is $3000 \mathrm{~K}$ is $(\sigma=5.7 \times$ $10^{-8} \mathrm{~W} \mathrm{~m}^{-2} \mathrm{~K}^{-4}$ )
solution:
According to Stefan's law
$$
E=\sigma T^4
$$
Heat radiated per unit area in 1 hour ( $3600 \mathrm{~s})$ is
$$
=5.7 \times 10^{-8} \times(300)^4 \times 3600=1.7 \times 10^{10} \mathrm{~J}
$$

It is heated, simultaneously, such that its temperature increases by <math><mi mathvariant="normal">Δ</mi><mi>T</mi></math>. The net change in its length is zero. Let <math><mi>l</mi></math> be the length of the rod, A its area of cross-section, <math><mi>Y</mi></math> its Young's modulus, and <math><mi>α</mi></math> its coefficient of linear expansion. Then, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math> is equal to:
question: A compressive force, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math> is applied at the two ends of a long thin steel rod. It is heated, simultaneously, such that its temperature increases by <math><mi mathvariant="normal">Δ</mi><mi>T</mi></math>. The net change in its length is zero. Let <math><mi>l</mi></math> be the length of the rod, A its area of cross-section, <math><mi>Y</mi></math> its Young's modulus, and <math><mi>α</mi></math> its coefficient of linear expansion. Then, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math> is equal to:
solution:
Net change in length =0

Thermal expansion. Δl =lα Δt

Y=FAΔll

Δll=FAY

Δl=FlAY

FlAY=l α Δt

F=AY α Δt

If coefficient of linear expansion and Young's modulus of steel are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1.2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>11</mn></msup><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> respectively, the force developed in the rail is approximately:
solution:
F=YAαΔt

⇒F=2×1011×40×10-4×1.2×10-5×10

⇒F=9.6×104≈1×105 N.

subject: Physics
q:
A gun fires a lead bullet of temperature 300 K into a wooden block. The bullet having melting temperature of 600 K penetrates into the block and melts down. If the total heat required for the process is 625 J , then the mass of the bullet is $\qquad$ grams.
(Latent heat of fusion of lead $=2.5 \times 10^4 \mathrm{JKg}^{-1}$ and specific heat capacity of lead $=125 \mathrm{JKg}^{-1}$ $\left.\mathrm{K}^{-1}\right)$
question:
A gun fires a lead bullet of temperature 300 K into a wooden block. The bullet having melting temperature of 600 K penetrates into the block and melts down. If the total heat required for the process is 625 J , then the mass of the bullet is $\qquad$ grams.
(Latent heat of fusion of lead $=2.5 \times 10^4 \mathrm{JKg}^{-1}$ and specific heat capacity of lead $=125 \mathrm{JKg}^{-1}$ $\left.\mathrm{K}^{-1}\right)$
solution: $\begin{aligned} & 625=\mathrm{ms} \Delta \mathrm{T}+\mathrm{mL} \\ & 625=\mathrm{m}\left[125 \times 300+2.5 \times 10^4\right] \\ & 625=\mathrm{m}[37500+25000] \\ & 625=\mathrm{m}[62500] \\ & \mathrm{m}=\frac{1}{100} \mathrm{~kg} \\ & \mathrm{M}=10 \text { grams }\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: An ideal gas is expanding such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><msup><mi>T</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>=</mo></math> constant. The coefficient of volume expansion of the gas is:
question: An ideal gas is expanding such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><msup><mi>T</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>=</mo></math> constant. The coefficient of volume expansion of the gas is:
solution:
γ=1VdVdt

PT3= constant

TVT3= Constant

T4 V-1=C

V-14 T3 dT+T4-1 V2 d V=0

dVdT=4T3/VT4/V2=4VT  ∴γ=4T

q: Two plates A and B have thermal conductivities <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>84</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>126</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> respectively. They have same surface area and same thickness. They are placed in contact along their surfaces. If the temperatures of the outer surfaces of  A and B are kept at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>100</mn><mo>∘</mo></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>0</mn><mo>∘</mo></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> respectively, then the temperature of the surface of contact in steady state is_____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mo>∘</mo></mmultiscripts></math>.
question: Two plates A and B have thermal conductivities <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>84</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>126</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> respectively. They have same surface area and same thickness. They are placed in contact along their surfaces. If the temperatures of the outer surfaces of  A and B are kept at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>100</mn><mo>∘</mo></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>0</mn><mo>∘</mo></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> respectively, then the temperature of the surface of contact in steady state is_____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mo>∘</mo></mmultiscripts></math>.
solution:
Let the temperature of the contact surface be T,

So, HA=HB

KAA'(TA-T)L=KBA'(T-TB)L⇒84(100-T)=126(T-0)⇒2(100-T)=3T⇒T=40 °C

subject: Physics
q: Water of volume 2 L in a closed container is heated with a coil of 1 kW. While water is heated, the container loses energy at a rate of 160 J/s. In how much time will the temperature of water rise from 27<sup>o</sup>C to 77<sup>o</sup>C ? (Specific heat of water is 4.2 kJ/kg and that of the container is negligible).
question: Water of volume 2 L in a closed container is heated with a coil of 1 kW. While water is heated, the container loses energy at a rate of 160 J/s. In how much time will the temperature of water rise from 27<sup>o</sup>C to 77<sup>o</sup>C ? (Specific heat of water is 4.2 kJ/kg and that of the container is negligible).
solution:
P = 1 0 0 0  watt

∴   Pin=1000-160 J/s=840 J/s

Now,   d Q dt = ms dT dt

∴    840=msdTdt

∴    dt = ms 8 4 0 dT

Integrating both sides we have;

t = ms 8 4 0 ∫ dT

    = 2 × 1 0 0 0 × 1 0 - 6 × 1 0 0 0 × 4.2 × 1 0 0 0 8 4 0 × 5 0

    = 5 0 0 sec

    = 8  min 2 0 sec

subject: Physics
q: A thin rod having a length of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and area of cross-section <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is suspended vertically from one end. The rod is cooled from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>210</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>160</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. After cooling, a mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> is attached at the lower end of the rod such that the length of rod again becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. Young's modulus and coefficient of linear expansion of the rod are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>11</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, respectively. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>kg</mi></math>. (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: A thin rod having a length of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and area of cross-section <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is suspended vertically from one end. The rod is cooled from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>210</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>160</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. After cooling, a mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> is attached at the lower end of the rod such that the length of rod again becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. Young's modulus and coefficient of linear expansion of the rod are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>11</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, respectively. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>kg</mi></math>. (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
Let Δl be the decease in length of rod due to decease in temperature

Then, Δl=lαΔT, here, coefficient of linear expansion is α=2×10-5 K-1 and ΔT=(483-433) K=50 K

Thus, Δl=1×2×10-5×50=10-3 m

Young's modulus is stated as  Y=FAΔll, here, F=Mg.

So, 2×1011=Mg×13×10-6×10-3

⇒Mg=2×1011×3×10-9=6×10-2

Thus, M=60 kg

subject: Physics
q: An aluminium and steel rods having same lengths and cross-sections are joined to make total length of 120 cm at $30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The coefficient of linear expansion of aluminium and steel are $24 \times 10^{-6} /{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $1.2 \times 10^{-5} /{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, respectively. The length of this composite rod when its temperature is raised to $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, is $\_\_\_\_$ cm.
question: An aluminium and steel rods having same lengths and cross-sections are joined to make total length of 120 cm at $30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The coefficient of linear expansion of aluminium and steel are $24 \times 10^{-6} /{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $1.2 \times 10^{-5} /{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, respectively. The length of this composite rod when its temperature is raised to $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, is $\_\_\_\_$ cm.
solution:
Total length at 30°C = 120 cm, each rod = 60 cm
$\alpha_{Al} = 24 \times 10^{-6}$/°C, $\alpha_{Steel} = 1.2 \times 10^{-5}$/°C
$\Delta T = 100 - 30 = 70$°C
$\Delta L_{Al} = 60 \times 24 \times 10^{-6} \times 70 = 0.1008$ cm
$\Delta L_{Steel} = 60 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-5} \times 70 = 0.0504$ cm
Total expansion $= 0.1008 + 0.0504 = 0.1512$ cm
Final length $= 120 + 0.1512 = 120.15$ cm

subject: Physics
q: A large cylindrical rod of length $L$ is made by joining two identical rods of copper and steel of length $\left(\frac{L}{2}\right)$ each. The rods are completely insulated from the surroundings. If the free end of copper rod is maintained at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and that of steel at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ then the temperature of junction is (Thermal conductivity of copper is 9 times that of steel)
question: A large cylindrical rod of length $L$ is made by joining two identical rods of copper and steel of length $\left(\frac{L}{2}\right)$ each. The rods are completely insulated from the surroundings. If the free end of copper rod is maintained at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and that of steel at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ then the temperature of junction is (Thermal conductivity of copper is 9 times that of steel)
solution: Let conductivity of steel $K_{\text {steel }}=k$ then from question Conductivity of copper $K_{\text {copper }}=9 k$ $$ \begin{aligned} & \theta_{\text {copper }}=100^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \\ & \theta_{\text {steel }}=0^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \\ & l_{\text {steel }}=l_{\text {copper }}=\frac{L}{2} \end{aligned} $$ From formula temperature of junction; $$ \begin{aligned} \theta & =\frac{K_{\text {copper }} \theta_{\text {copper }} l_{\text {steel }}+K_{\text {steel }} \theta_{\text {steel }} l_{\text {copper }}}{K_{\text {copper }} l_{\text {steel }}+K_{\text {steel }} l_{\text {copper }}} \\ & =\frac{9 k \times 100 \times \frac{L}{2}+k \times 0 \times \frac{L}{2}}{9 k \times \frac{L}{2}+k \times \frac{L}{2}} \\ & =\frac{\frac{900}{2} k L}{\frac{10 k L}{2}}=90^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \end{aligned} $$

subject: Physics
q: $100 \mathrm{~g}$ of water is heated from $30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Ignoring the slight expansion of the water, the change in its internal energy is (specific heat of water is $4148 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{kg} / \mathrm{K}$ ):
question: $100 \mathrm{~g}$ of water is heated from $30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Ignoring the slight expansion of the water, the change in its internal energy is (specific heat of water is $4148 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{kg} / \mathrm{K}$ ):
solution:
$\Delta \mathrm{Q}=\Delta \mathrm{U}+\Delta \mathrm{W}$ (ignoring expansion)
$\Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{ms} \Delta \mathrm{T}=0.1 \times 4.184 \times 20=8.368 \mathrm{~kJ}$

subject: Physics
q: Consider a rectangular sheet of solid material of length $\ell=9 \mathrm{~cm}$ and width $\mathrm{d}=4 \mathrm{~cm}$. The coefficient of linear expansion is $\alpha=3.1 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$ at room temperature and one atmospheric pressure. The mass of sheet $\mathrm{m}=0.1 \mathrm{~kg}$ and the specific heat capacity $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{v}}=900 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$. If the amount of heat supplied to the material is $8.1 \times 10^2 \mathrm{~J}$ then change in area of the rectangular sheet is :-
question: Consider a rectangular sheet of solid material of length $\ell=9 \mathrm{~cm}$ and width $\mathrm{d}=4 \mathrm{~cm}$. The coefficient of linear expansion is $\alpha=3.1 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$ at room temperature and one atmospheric pressure. The mass of sheet $\mathrm{m}=0.1 \mathrm{~kg}$ and the specific heat capacity $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{v}}=900 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$. If the amount of heat supplied to the material is $8.1 \times 10^2 \mathrm{~J}$ then change in area of the rectangular sheet is :-
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \Delta \mathrm{Q}=\mathrm{ms} \Delta \mathrm{T} \\ & 8.1 \times 10^2=0.1 \times 900 \times \Delta \mathrm{T} \\ & \Delta \mathrm{A}=\mathrm{A}_0 2 \alpha \Delta \mathrm{~T}=2.0 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}^2\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Two metallic blocks M1 and M2 of same area of cross-section are connected to each other (as shown in figure). If the thermal conductivity of M2 is K then the thermal conductivity of M1 will be : [Assume steady state heat conduction]
question: Two metallic blocks M1 and M2 of same area of cross-section are connected to each other (as shown in figure). If the thermal conductivity of M2 is K then the thermal conductivity of M1 will be : [Assume steady state heat conduction]
solution:
Let thermal conductivity of M1 be K'.

Using heat current relation for steady state conduction,

K'A100-8016=KA80-08

⇒K'×2016=K×808⇒K'=8K

subject: Physics
q:
The heat extracted out of $x$ gram of water initially at $50°C$ to cool it down to $0°C$ is sufficient to evaporate $(1000 - x)$ gram of water also initially at $50°C$. The value of $x$ (closest integer) is _______.
(Take latent heat of water $2256\text{ kJ/kg.K}$, specific heat capacity of water $4200\text{ J/kg.K}$)
question:
The heat extracted out of $x$ gram of water initially at $50°C$ to cool it down to $0°C$ is sufficient to evaporate $(1000 - x)$ gram of water also initially at $50°C$. The value of $x$ (closest integer) is _______.
(Take latent heat of water $2256\text{ kJ/kg.K}$, specific heat capacity of water $4200\text{ J/kg.K}$)
solution:
Heat extracted to cool $x$ gram of water from $50^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $0^{\circ}\text{C}$ is given by:

$Q_1 = m_1 c \Delta T_1$

$Q_1 = x \times 4.2 \times (50 - 0) = 210x \text{ J}$

Heat required to raise the temperature of $(1000 - x)$ gram of water from $50^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $100^{\circ}\text{C}$ and evaporate it is given by:

$Q_2 = m_2 c \Delta T_2 + m_2 L$

$Q_2 = (1000 - x) \times [4.2 \times (100 - 50) + 2256]$

$Q_2 = (1000 - x) \times (210 + 2256) = 2466(1000 - x) \text{ J}$

Equating the heat extracted and the heat required:

$Q_1 = Q_2$

$210x = 2466(1000 - x)$

$210x = 2466000 - 2466x$

$2676x = 2466000$

$x = \dfrac{2466000}{2676} \approx 921.52$

The closest integer value for $x$ is $922$.

Answer: $922$

subject: Physics
q: Two conductors have the same resistances at 0 °C but their temperature coefficients of resistance are α1 and α2. The respective temperature coefficients for their series and parallel combinations are :
question: Two conductors have the same resistances at 0 °C but their temperature coefficients of resistance are α1 and α2. The respective temperature coefficients for their series and parallel combinations are :
solution:
Change in resistance due to temperature is given by, R'=R1+α∆T

For Series Combination:

Req=R1+R2

⇒2R1+αeq∆T=R1+α1∆T+R1+α2∆T

⇒2R1+αeq∆T=2R+α1+α2R∆T

⇒αeq=α1+α22

For Parallel Combination:

1Req=1R1+1R2

⇒1R21+αeq∆T=1R1+α1∆T+1R1+α2∆T

⇒21+αeq∆T=11+α1∆T+11+α2∆T

⇒21+αeq∆T=1+α2∆T+1+α1∆T1+α1∆T1+α2∆T

⇒21+α1∆T1+α2∆T=2+α1+α2∆T1+αeq∆T

⇒21+α1∆T+α2∆T+α1α2∆T=2+2αeq∆T+α1+α2∆T+αeqα1+α2∆T2

Neglecting small terms

2+2α1+α2∆T=2+2αeq∆T+α1+α2∆T

⇒α1+α2∆T=2αeq∆T

⇒αeq=α1+α22

subject: Physics
q:
Two cylindrical rods A and B made of different materials, are joined in a straight line. The ratio of lengths, radii and thermal conductivities of these rods are :
$\frac{\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\frac{1}{2}, \frac{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{B}}}=2$ and $\frac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\frac{1}{2}$. The free ends of rods A and B are maintained at $400 \mathrm{~K}, 200 \mathrm{~K}$, respectively. The temperature of rods interface is ________ K, when equilibrium is established.
question:
Two cylindrical rods A and B made of different materials, are joined in a straight line. The ratio of lengths, radii and thermal conductivities of these rods are :
$\frac{\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\frac{1}{2}, \frac{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{B}}}=2$ and $\frac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\frac{1}{2}$. The free ends of rods A and B are maintained at $400 \mathrm{~K}, 200 \mathrm{~K}$, respectively. The temperature of rods interface is ________ K, when equilibrium is established.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{R}_1=\frac{\ell_1}{\mathrm{~K}_1 \mathrm{~A}_1}, \mathrm{R}_2=\frac{\ell_2}{\mathrm{~K}_2 \mathrm{~A}_2} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{dQ}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{T}}{\mathrm{R}} \\ & \left(\frac{\mathrm{dQ}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right)_1=\left(\frac{\mathrm{dQ}}{\mu}\right)_2 \\ & \frac{400-\mathrm{T}}{\mathrm{R}_1}=\frac{\mathrm{T}-200}{\mathrm{R}_2} \\ & \frac{400-\mathrm{T}}{\mathrm{T}-200}=\frac{\mathrm{R}_1}{\mathrm{R}_2}=\left(\frac{\ell_1}{\ell_2}\right)\left(\frac{\mathrm{r}_2}{\mathrm{r}_1}\right)^2 \times \frac{\mathrm{K}_2}{\mathrm{~K}_1} \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \times\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2 \times 2 \\ & =\left(\frac{1}{4}\right) \\ & \frac{400-\mathrm{T}}{\mathrm{T}-200}=\frac{1}{4} \\ & 1600-4 \mathrm{~T}=\mathrm{T}-200 \\ & 5 \mathrm{~T}=1800 \\ & \mathrm{~T}=360 \mathrm{~K}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A cylinder of radius <math><mi>R</mi></math> is surrounded by a cylindrical shell of inner radius <math><mi>R</mi></math> and outer radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>R</mi></math>. The thermal conductivity of the material of the inner cylinder is <math><msub><mrow><mi>K</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and that of the outer cylinder is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>K</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. Assuming no loss of heat, the effective thermal conductivity of the system for heat flowing along the length of the cylinder is:
question: A cylinder of radius <math><mi>R</mi></math> is surrounded by a cylindrical shell of inner radius <math><mi>R</mi></math> and outer radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>R</mi></math>. The thermal conductivity of the material of the inner cylinder is <math><msub><mrow><mi>K</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and that of the outer cylinder is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>K</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. Assuming no loss of heat, the effective thermal conductivity of the system for heat flowing along the length of the cylinder is:
solution:
Let q1 and q2 be the rate of flow of heat through inner part from outer part respecting net flow of heat


q=q1+q2

=K1πR2l.ΔT+K2.π2R2-R2l.ΔT

=πR2lΔTK1+3K2

If the cylinder is replaced by a single material of thermal conductivity K then

q=K.2R2l.ΔT

On comparison,

K=K1+3K24.

subject: Physics
q: In an experiment, a sphere of aluminium of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is heated up to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> . Immediately, it is put into water of volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cc</mi><mo> </mo></math>at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>27</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> kept in a calorimeter of water equivalent to<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>025</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> . The final temperature of the system is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>40</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> . The specific heat of the aluminium is(take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Joule</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>calorie</mi></math> )
question: In an experiment, a sphere of aluminium of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is heated up to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> . Immediately, it is put into water of volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cc</mi><mo> </mo></math>at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>27</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> kept in a calorimeter of water equivalent to<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>025</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> . The final temperature of the system is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>40</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> . The specific heat of the aluminium is(take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Joule</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>calorie</mi></math> )
solution:
Let S be the specific heat of aluminium

Q given = Q used

⇒1000×0.2×S×(150−40)=150×1×(40−27)+25×1×(40−27)

⇒200×S×110=150×13+25×13

⇒S=13×175200×110=0.1034 cal g-1 °C

⇒S=103.4 ×4.2 J kg-1 °C=434 J kg-1°C

subject: Physics
q: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of iron nail is hit by a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> hammer striking at a velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>ms</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. What will be the rise in the temperature of the nail if one fourth of energy of the hammer goes into heating the nail ? [Specific heat capacity of iron <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>42</mn><msup><mi>Jg</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mo>∘</mo></mmultiscripts><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
question: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of iron nail is hit by a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> hammer striking at a velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>ms</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. What will be the rise in the temperature of the nail if one fourth of energy of the hammer goes into heating the nail ? [Specific heat capacity of iron <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>42</mn><msup><mi>Jg</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mo>∘</mo></mmultiscripts><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
solution:
If we heat-up an object, then, the energy is used to either change the temperature of the body or to change the state of the body.
If it changes the temperature, then,

Q = mS∆T
where, Q is the heat given, m is the mass and ∆T is the change in temperature.

We know that a moving object has an energy in the form of kinetic energy, i.e.,
KE=12mv2

When that moving object strikes another object, it loses some energy which changes into the heat energy and this heat energy increases the temperature of the object.
Given a hammer of 1.5 kg is moving with 60 m s-1.
Kinetic energy =12mv2 = 12(1.5) (60)2 = 2700 J
One-fourth of kinetic energy changes into heat energy.
⇒   14(KE) = Heat energy
⇒   14(KE) = m(S)(∆T)
⇒   14(2700) =(100) (0.42) (∆T)
⇒   ∆T=16.07°C

subject: Physics
q:
1 kg of water, at 20 °C is heated in an electric kettle whose heating element has a mean (temperature averaged) resistance of 20 Ω. The rms voltage in the mains is 200 V. Ignoring heat loss from the kettle, time taken for water to evaporate fully is close to
[ Specific heat of water =4200 J kg-1 °C-1 Latent heat of water =2260 kJ kg-1 ]
question:
1 kg of water, at 20 °C is heated in an electric kettle whose heating element has a mean (temperature averaged) resistance of 20 Ω. The rms voltage in the mains is 200 V. Ignoring heat loss from the kettle, time taken for water to evaporate fully is close to
[ Specific heat of water =4200 J kg-1 °C-1 Latent heat of water =2260 kJ kg-1 ]
solution:
Given specific heat of the water 4200 J kg-1 °C-1

Latent heat of the water =2260  kJ kg-1  

Q=P×t

Q=mcΔT+mL

⇒P×t=mcΔT+mL

⇒Vrms2R×t=mcΔT+mL

⇒200220×t=4200×80+2260×103

⇒t=1298 s≃22 min

subject: Physics
q:
At 40°C, a brass wire of 1 mm radius is hung from the ceiling. A small mass, M is hung from the free end of the wire. When the wire is cooled down from 40°C  to 20°C it regains its original length of 0.2 m. The value of M is close to:
(Coefficient of linear expansion and Young’s modulus of brass are 10-5/°C and 1011 N/m2, respectively; g=10 m s-2 )
question:
At 40°C, a brass wire of 1 mm radius is hung from the ceiling. A small mass, M is hung from the free end of the wire. When the wire is cooled down from 40°C  to 20°C it regains its original length of 0.2 m. The value of M is close to:
(Coefficient of linear expansion and Young’s modulus of brass are 10-5/°C and 1011 N/m2, respectively; g=10 m s-2 )
solution:
Mg=AYlΔl
Δll=αΔT
Mg=AYαΔT
M=AYαΔTg=π×0.0012×1011×10-5×2010=2π
Closest value of M is 9 kg.

subject: Physics
q: Two metallic wires of identical dimensions are connected is series. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>σ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>σ</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are the conductivities of the these wires respectively, the effective conductivity of the combination is :
question: Two metallic wires of identical dimensions are connected is series. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>σ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>σ</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are the conductivities of the these wires respectively, the effective conductivity of the combination is :
solution:
Let length of wire be l,
Area of wire as A
For equivalent wire length =2l & area will be A

As the wires are connected in series combination, the thermal resistance

Req=R1+R2

⇒2lσeq A=lσ1 A+lσ1 A

⇒2σeq =1σ1+1σ2⇒σeq =2σ1σ2σ1+σ2

subject: Physics
q: A body takes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>min</mi></math> to cool from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>61</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>59</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the temperature of the surroundings is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the time taken by the body to cool from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>51</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>49</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is:
question: A body takes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>min</mi></math> to cool from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>61</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>59</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the temperature of the surroundings is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the time taken by the body to cool from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>51</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>49</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is:
solution:
ΔTΔt=KTt-TS Tt= average temperature

TS= surrounding temperature

61-594=K61+592-30  ...(i)

51-49t=K51+492-30  ...ii

Divide (1) and (2)

t4=60-3050-30=3020

So, t=6 min

subject: Physics
q: If the temperature of the sun were to increase from $T$ to $2 T$ and its radius from $R$ to $2 R$, then the ratio of the radiant energy received on earth to what it was previously will be
question: If the temperature of the sun were to increase from $T$ to $2 T$ and its radius from $R$ to $2 R$, then the ratio of the radiant energy received on earth to what it was previously will be
solution:
According to stefan's law,
$P \propto A T^4$ and $A \propto r^2$
$P \propto r^2 T^4$

subject: Physics
q: An experiment takes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>min</mi></math> to raise the temperature of water in a container from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>0</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>100</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and another <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>55</mn><mo> </mo><mi>min</mi></math> to convert it totally into steam by a heater supplying heat at a uniform rate. Neglecting the specific heat of the container and taking specific heat of the water to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cal</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>∘</mo></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> , the heat of vaporization according to this experiment will come out to be:
question: An experiment takes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>min</mi></math> to raise the temperature of water in a container from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>0</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>100</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and another <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>55</mn><mo> </mo><mi>min</mi></math> to convert it totally into steam by a heater supplying heat at a uniform rate. Neglecting the specific heat of the container and taking specific heat of the water to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cal</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>∘</mo></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> , the heat of vaporization according to this experiment will come out to be:
solution: Let the constant state of supply of heat per unit time <math><mo>=</mo><mi>λ</mi></math>. So according to the first condition. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>m</mi><mi>c</mi><mo>×</mo><mn>100</mn><mi> </mi><mo>⇒</mo><mfrac><mi>λ</mi><mi>m</mi></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>100</mn><mi>C</mi></mrow><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></mfrac><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>)</mo></math>, and according to the second condition, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>m</mi><msub><mi>L</mi><mi>f</mi></msub><mo>⇒</mo><mfrac><mi>λ</mi><mi>m</mi></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><msub><mi>L</mi><mi>f</mi></msub><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfrac><mo> </mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>(</mo><mi>ii</mi><mo>)</mo></math>. By <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>)</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>ii</mi><mo>)</mo></math> <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>100</mn><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>L</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>f</mi></mrow></msub></mrow><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfrac></math><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>⇒</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn><mi>cal</mi><mo>×</mo><mn>100</mn></mrow><mn>10</mn></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><msub><mi>L</mi><mi>f</mi></msub><mn>55</mn></mfrac><mo>⇒</mo><msub><mi>L</mi><mi>f</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>550</mn><mi> </mi><mi>cal</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>

subject: Physics
q: A cylinder of fixed capacity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>44</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn></math> litres contains helium gas at standard temperature and pressure. The amount of heat needed to raise the temperature of gas in the cylinder by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> will be (Given gas constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: A cylinder of fixed capacity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>44</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn></math> litres contains helium gas at standard temperature and pressure. The amount of heat needed to raise the temperature of gas in the cylinder by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> will be (Given gas constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
At STP volume occupied by one mole of a gas is 22.4 L. Therefore, number of moles are,  n=44.822.4=2 mol.

The process is isochoric process as the volume of the cylinder remains fixed and helium is a monatomic gas. Therefore, amount of heat needed will be,

Q=nCvΔT=232R20=3×8.3×20=498 J

subject: Physics
q: A hole is drilled in a metal sheet. At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>, the diameter of hole is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. When the sheet is heated to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>177</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>, the change in the diameter of hole is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> . The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> will be _____, if coefficient of linear expansion of the metal is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo>/</mo><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mo>∘</mo></mmultiscripts></math>
question: A hole is drilled in a metal sheet. At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>, the diameter of hole is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. When the sheet is heated to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>177</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>, the change in the diameter of hole is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> . The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> will be _____, if coefficient of linear expansion of the metal is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo>/</mo><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mo>∘</mo></mmultiscripts></math>
solution:
Expansion of hole will be similar to that of an imaginary disc.

Let diameter be d0 at 27 °C and d' at 177 °C. Therefore, we can write

d'=d01+αΔT

Therefore, change in diameter will be

⇒d'-d0=5×1.6×10-5×177-27

=12×10-3 cm

Hence, required value of d=12.

subject: Physics
q: The specific heat of water <math><mrow><mo>=</mo><mn>4200</mn><mtext> J</mtext></mrow></math> <math><mrow><msup><mrow><mtext>kg</mtext></mrow><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mtext> K</mtext></mrow><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></math> and the latent heat of ice <math><mrow><mo>=</mo><mn>3.4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mn>5</mn></msup><mtext> J</mtext></mrow></math> k<math><mrow><msup><mtext>g</mtext><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></math>. <math><mrow><mn>100</mn></mrow></math> grams of ice at <math><mrow><mn>0</mn><msup><mtext> </mtext><mtext>o</mtext></msup><mtext>C</mtext></mrow></math> is placed in <math><mrow><mn>200</mn><mtext> g</mtext></mrow></math> of water at <math><mrow><mn>25</mn><msup><mtext> </mtext><mtext>o</mtext></msup><mtext>C</mtext></mrow></math>. The amount of ice that will melt as the temperature of water reaches <math><mrow><mn>0</mn><msup><mtext> </mtext><mtext>o</mtext></msup><mtext>C</mtext></mrow></math> is close to (in grams)
question: The specific heat of water <math><mrow><mo>=</mo><mn>4200</mn><mtext> J</mtext></mrow></math> <math><mrow><msup><mrow><mtext>kg</mtext></mrow><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mtext> K</mtext></mrow><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></math> and the latent heat of ice <math><mrow><mo>=</mo><mn>3.4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mn>5</mn></msup><mtext> J</mtext></mrow></math> k<math><mrow><msup><mtext>g</mtext><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></math>. <math><mrow><mn>100</mn></mrow></math> grams of ice at <math><mrow><mn>0</mn><msup><mtext> </mtext><mtext>o</mtext></msup><mtext>C</mtext></mrow></math> is placed in <math><mrow><mn>200</mn><mtext> g</mtext></mrow></math> of water at <math><mrow><mn>25</mn><msup><mtext> </mtext><mtext>o</mtext></msup><mtext>C</mtext></mrow></math>. The amount of ice that will melt as the temperature of water reaches <math><mrow><mn>0</mn><msup><mtext> </mtext><mtext>o</mtext></msup><mtext>C</mtext></mrow></math> is close to (in grams)
solution:
MSΔT=Ml

2001000×4200×25=m×340×103

m=61.7
